title: How Every's Team Used AI to Ship Its Biggest Launch Ever

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We generated the largest subscription revenue increase in Everest's history last week.
We did it by launching this thing called the Builder Pack.
Basically, we partnered with a bunch of the AI tools we use every day, and subscribers to the Builder Pack get discounts and credits to those tools.
So thousand dollars in Codex credits, twelve months of free cursor access,

paragraphs.zh: 上周，我们创造了 Everest 历史上最大的一次订阅收入增长。我们是通过推出一个叫 Builder Pack 的东西做到的。基本上，我们和一大批我们每天都在用的 AI 工具达成了合作，而订阅 Builder Pack 的用户可以拿到这些工具的折扣和 credits（额度）。比如价值一千美元的 Codex credits、十二个月免费的 cursor 使用权限，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: all that kind of stuff.

paragraphs.zh: ] 诸如此类的这些福利。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: The Builder Pack is awesome, but I think the actual story behind it, like how we did it, why we did it, how it generated so much revenue, is really interesting.
For this episode, I had the team who worked on the Builder Pack.
Everything from conception all the way to the full execution at launch come to the show to share how we did it and what we learned.
I hope you like it.

paragraphs.zh: Builder Pack 很棒，但我觉得它背后真正的故事——比如我们是怎么做出来的、为什么要这么做、它为什么能带来这么多收入——其实特别有意思。这一期里，我请来了参与 Builder Pack 的团队成员。从最初构想到上线时的完整执行，他们都会来节目里分享我们是怎么做的，以及我们学到了什么。希望你会喜欢。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Hello, everyone, and welcome to AI and I. This is a slightly different version of the pod today, where we've got myself, Josh, Austin, and Douglas here, and we're missing Daniel.
We're gonna be taking you on a journey today, introducing you to All Access in the builder pack.

And this is a new tier at Every that we decided to launch recently, and we would just wanna talk about why we decided to build it, why it's important, how we use it, and what it means to be a builder today.
All of which all of us are are are builders and use these tools every day.

paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎来到 AI and I。今天这期播客会稍微有点不一样，现场有我自己、Josh、Austin 和 Douglas，不过 Daniel 缺席了。今天我们会带大家做一次深入了解，介绍 All Access 和 Builder Pack。这是 Every 最近决定推出的一个新 tier（等级方案），我们想聊聊为什么决定做它、它为什么重要、我们自己怎么使用它，以及在今天这个时代，成为一个 builder（构建者）意味着什么。我们几个人其实都是 builder，而且每天都在使用这些工具。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: So, yeah, hopefully by the end of this, there'll be like one nugget that you can take out of this and and and use in in your everyday.
So I guess, like, to introduce us or to introduce this this pod, first, I'm gonna introduce All Access.
We launched it Tuesday on the fourteenth.
It was a big launch for us.
Big endeavor across the whole company.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，希望到最后，你至少能从这里带走一个有用的点，并且用到你的日常工作里。那我想，先介绍一下我们，或者说先介绍一下这期播客的主题。首先我要介绍的是 All Access。我们在 14 号星期二发布了它。对我们来说，这是一次很大的发布，也是一次涉及全公司的重大项目。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: All Access is a new tier.
At every it's a $625 annual plan that gives you access to the builder pack, which is 10 benefits, exclusive benefits from all AI companies that you know and love, that enable you to go build.
And adopt some of these tools in ways that you may not have been able to before.
And All Access includes a couple other things.

It includes access to office hours that we're hosting that are that are exclusive to All Access members, unlimited use of Quora and Spiral, and we have a couple other things up our sleeve that we're gonna be launching in the next few months as well.

paragraphs.zh: All Access 是一个新的 tier。在 Every，它是一个 625 美元的年度方案，购买后你可以获得 Builder Pack 的访问权限。Builder Pack 包含 10 项福利，都是来自那些你熟悉且喜爱的 AI 公司提供的专属福利，帮助你真正开始构建，并以你以前可能做不到的方式采用这些工具。All Access 还包含另外几项内容：包括参加我们举办的 office hours（办公答疑时段）的权限，这些是 All Access 会员专属的；还包括对 Quora 和 Spiral 的无限使用；另外，我们还有一些别的内容正在筹备中，也会在接下来的几个月里推出。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: But we really wanna talk about the Builder Pack, because that's like probably the meat of of All Access.

And the builder pack is 10 soon to be more exclusive benefits to AI tools that the every team uses every day, so that you can get access to these tools and use them, you know, for free for a while and and get comfortable with using them in your stack.
It's it's it's early access, exclusive access to the EveryStack.
This is the team that that brought it to life.
But, Yash, do you use the builder path?

paragraphs.zh: 但我们真正想重点聊的是 Builder Pack，因为它大概算是 All Access 最核心的部分。Builder Pack 目前包含 10 项、未来还会继续增加的专属福利，面向 Every 团队每天都在使用的 AI 工具。这样你就可以获得这些工具的使用权限，免费用上一段时间，并逐渐熟悉如何把它们整合进你的 stack（技术栈）里。它可以说是对 EveryStack 的 early access（抢先体验）和 exclusive access（专属访问）。而这就是把它真正做出来的团队。不过，Yash，你会用 builder path 吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Yeah.
Context.
I'm Yash.
I run one of the bot like, I'm just Yeah.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 会。补充一下背景，我是 Yash。我负责其中一个 bot（机器人）……算是，我就是……对，对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Who are you?

paragraphs.zh: 你是谁？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Who who am I?
Wow.
I ask that myself that every day, but I run one of the products at every called Sparkle, and I also work on the growth team now.
A large part of my work is using Cloud.
I know at every year, like Codec stands, but I'm still very Cloud.

paragraphs.zh: 我是谁来着？哇。这个问题我自己每天都在问，不过我在 Every 负责一个叫 Sparkle 的产品，现在也在 growth team 工作。我的工作很大一部分都在用 Claude。我知道在 Every，像 Codecs 之类的也很重要，但我现在还是非常依赖 Claude。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: I've been using Cloud for the last one year.
And this involves like, I built out the entire product, like I think 15 rebuilt Sparkle 15 times using Cloud and Posthoc.
So those are the tools I reach out for the most.
And recently I joined the growth team at Every.
And growth meant a lot of AB testing, a lot of segmentation and other stuff.

paragraphs.zh: 过去这一年我一直在用 Claude。这包括，比如我把整个产品都搭起来了，我想我大概用 Claude 和 PostHog 把 Sparkle 重建了 15 次。所以这些就是我最常用的工具。最近我加入了 Every 的 growth team。而 growth 意味着大量的 AB testing，以及大量的用户分群和其他事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: And again, I started optimizing postdoc, but I find it really annoying to open like dashboards and set a lot of these things up.
And that's where my setup with Claude helps a lot where I have like custom skills built out.
And essentially my plan is in the next few weeks to automate the entire AB testing pipeline.
Like a lot of the work that I spent doing for the first one month at the growth team, I'm like, this is very boring because it's it's very straightforward.
Like, should be working on interesting more interesting problems and I should not be clicking buttons and figuring out what's the right audience size and stuff like that.

paragraphs.zh: 然后，我又开始优化 PostHog，但我发现老是打开各种 dashboard、设置一大堆这类东西，真的很烦。这时候我和 Claude 的那套配置就特别有帮助，我给它做了一些 custom skills。基本上，我的计划是在接下来几周把整条 AB testing 流水线完全自动化。像我在 growth team 第一个月花时间做的很多工作，我都会觉得，这太无聊了，因为它其实非常直接。我们应该去做更有意思的问题，而不是一直点按钮、算什么 audience size 才合适之类的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Like, Claude is so much better at that.
I have to prompt it.
I have to figure out, okay.
What's the right audience size?
When should an experiment go from a 10% AB testing cohort to 50%?

paragraphs.zh: 比如，Claude 在这方面就强太多了。我只需要 prompt（提示）它，然后搞清楚，好，合适的 audience size 是多少？一个实验应该在什么时候从 10% 的 AB testing cohort（分组）扩大到 50%？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Like, should I be doing that manually?
It's I I feel that it's a lot of fake work, and we like to spend a lot of time observing these things, like setting up, like, setting up an experiment every time.
It so boring to me.
I'm like, the only way I can set up do more experiments is automating the entire pipeline.
So I spend a lot of

paragraphs.zh: 这种事真的该我手动做吗？我觉得这里面很多都是 fake work（伪工作），而且我们总是花很多时间在观察和搭这些东西上，比如每次都要重新设置一个实验。这对我来说太无聊了。我会想，我能做更多实验的唯一办法，就是把整个流水线自动化。所以我花了很多

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: time So so what's what's interesting to you is less actually the work.
The work is no longer setting up the pipeline to be able to do it.
It's actually what should I test?
Yeah.
And then did it work?

paragraphs.zh: 时间——所以，对你来说真正有意思的，其实已经不太是这份工作本身了。工作不再是把流水线搭起来、让这件事能做，而是：我应该测试什么？对。然后它到底有没有效果？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Great.
Implement it.
What's the next test?

paragraphs.zh: 很好。那就实施它。然后下一个测试是什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Yeah.
I should be like, I'm more interested in the ideas.
Like, oh, should we do a homepage takeover?
Yeah.
Who should we show that homepage takeover to?

paragraphs.zh: 对。我应该是那种——我对想法本身更感兴趣。比如，哦，我们要不要做一个 homepage takeover？对。那我们应该把这个 homepage takeover 展示给谁？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Versus actually setting that up is not that interesting.
Like, we have so many experiments live right now, which are like, when should we cut off the paywall?
When should we show the paywall?
Those are way more interesting and those are also like more human psychology.
And I can I have more fun doing that?

paragraphs.zh: 相比之下，真正把它搭起来这件事就没那么有意思了。比如，我们现在同时在线跑着很多 experiment（实验），像是：我们应该在什么时候切断 paywall（付费墙）？应该在什么时候展示 paywall？这些就有意思得多，而且也更偏向于 human psychology（人类心理）。而且我做这个也会更有乐趣，不是吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Mhmm.

paragraphs.zh: 嗯。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: And and and I personally feel like a large part of our work at Avery is, like, everyone is very secure in what they do, and so we are okay with automating our jobs.
Like, I spent one month doing these things, and I'm like, okay.
I'm done.
Mhmm.
Like, I wanna do other things now.

paragraphs.zh: 而且，而且，而且我个人觉得，我们在 Avery 的工作有很大一部分是：每个人都对自己做的事情很有安全感，所以我们可以接受把自己的工作自动化。比如，我花了一个月做这些事，然后我就会想，行，可以了。我做完了。嗯哼。比如，我现在想去做别的事情了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: And if it was some other organization, I'm gonna try to stretch this out for, like, a year.

paragraphs.zh: 但如果是在别的组织里，我可能会想办法把这件事拖长到一年左右。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Like, what what I did You wanna you wanna move on to the next thing that you tap?

paragraphs.zh: 就像，我做的那些——你会想继续到下一个你上手的东西，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Yeah.
Because that's fun, and that's why we are always at the edge of AI.
Right?
Like, we are like, yeah.
This is done, guys.

paragraphs.zh: 对。因为那样很有趣，这也是为什么我们总是在 AI 的前沿，对吧？比如，我们会说，行，这个已经做完了，各位。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Like, what's next?
Like, what's more interesting?
Okay.
Is there a better model for this?

paragraphs.zh: 那接下来呢？什么更有意思？好，那这件事有没有更好的 model（模型）？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Is So so what in the builder pack Yeah.
You use every day?

paragraphs.zh: 那么，所以在 builder pack 里——对。你每天都会用什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Every day, it's Cursor.
I use Cursor because they have cloud regions, which are really good.
We did a lot a bunch of Fable five optimizations because I was working on some other task.
I did not wanna mess with my setup.
So cloud agents just spin off a cloud server and run there and give get get me the results back.

paragraphs.zh: 我每天都用的是 Cursor。我用 Cursor 是因为他们有 cloud regions（云区域），这点真的很好。我们之前做了很多 Fable five 的优化，因为当时我在处理别的任务。我不想折腾我现有的 setup（配置环境）。所以 cloud agents（云端 agent）可以直接拉起一台 cloud server（云服务器）在那边运行，然后把结果返回给我。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: And they have a really good open source model, which is really cheap, like, unique composer, which I love.
So that's something I use.
I used Framer a lot as well while I was building the Sparkle site.
It it has a it it has good MCP support, I could change the change the headlines, see it, like experiment more directly without actually going into framework and setting.
Like, every time it's very annoying to me.

paragraphs.zh: 而且他们有一个非常好的 open source model（开源模型），价格很便宜，比如 unique composer，我很喜欢。所以这是我会用的东西。我在做 Sparkle site 的时候也用了很多 Framer。它有很好的 MCP support（MCP 支持），我可以直接改 headline（标题）并立刻看到效果，更直接地做实验，而不用每次都进 framework（框架）里去设置。那对我来说每次都很烦。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Like, I'm setting a lot of these things up the right font size for mobile and stuff like that.
Yeah.
This is how you like like, I I used to do this, like, twenty years back.
Like, today, I should not be spending my time on these things.
And I would say Claude Max is my go to.

paragraphs.zh: 比如我还得去设置很多这种东西，像 mobile 端合适的 font size（字体大小）之类的。对，这就是你会去做的那种事——我二十年前确实会这样做。但放到今天，我不该把时间花在这些事情上。我会说 Claude Max 是我的首选。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: I Yeah.
Like, I I feel it's insane.
Yeah.
That's my stack.
It's tough.

paragraphs.zh: 我……对，我觉得这简直离谱。对，这就是我的 stack（技术栈）。挺难的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Yeah.
And post hoc.
Yeah.
I use a lot of these products, but it's so cheap.
Like, I feel that Cloudmax subscription is really underrated.

paragraphs.zh: 对，还有 post hoc。对，我用了很多这类产品，但它其实非常便宜。比如我觉得 Cloudmax subscription（Cloudmax 订阅）真的被严重低估了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Like, I'd pay way more for it.
Right.
I couldn't work without it.

paragraphs.zh: 我愿意为它付更多钱。真的，没有它我根本没法工作。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Right.
Anthropic.
I hope no one from Anthropic is watching this.

paragraphs.zh: 对，Anthropic。希望没有 Anthropic 的人在看这个。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 4

sentences.text: Yeah.
Get the d, guys.
Get get get get the Builder pack.

paragraphs.zh: 对，去买 d，伙计们。去买买买买那个 Builder pack。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: I I like the way Yasha is describing, one potential approach to work, which is like, there there's this there's this approach to being a general manager in the NBA, which is like, just keep trading for future draft picks to be like, we're on an eight year time horizon for contention.

And there is for someone as, like, skilled as Josh at engineering, go to market engineering growth, you can use these tools, this stack that he has to to, like, operate at the same level and, like, a set it and forget it mode and kind of I would be like, well, Yash like, I I run the growth team.
I look at what Yash is doing.
I'd be like, Yash is another experiment, and it, like, moves the needle by 2%.
I guess he's on it.

paragraphs.zh: 我喜欢 Yasha 描述的一种潜在工作方式，有点像 NBA 里当 general manager 的一种思路：不断交易、换取未来的 draft picks，就像在说“我们争冠的时间线是八年”。而对于像 Josh 这样在 engineering、go to market、engineering growth 方面都非常有能力的人来说，你可以用他手上的这些工具、这套 stack（技术栈），以同样的水准运行工作，进入一种 set it and forget it（设好后基本自动运行）的模式。某种程度上我会想，如果我是 Yash——我是带 growth team 的——我看 Yash 在做什么时，可能会觉得：“Yash 又是另一个 experiment（实验），它大概把指标推进了 2%，行吧，那他应该在持续跑这件事。”

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: And, like, he knows how to set up that that Claude code and post hoc loop.
But the way that he works instead, which is exciting, is that after he does that, he looks at how can we win right now?
How can I be creative and competitive to find the next thing?

And how can I use these always on, like, loop and architecture based automations to make it so that Yash can do the thing that, like, lights them up, which is I can see Yash in Slack being like, oh, what if we, like, made a three hour time constrained discount specifically targeted to these users using this video, building this thing in Framer?
And that's, this kind of stuff has always been the case, right?

paragraphs.zh: 而且，他也知道怎么搭起 Claude code 和 post hoc loop。但真正令人兴奋的是，他实际的工作方式不是停在那里，而是在做完这些之后，会继续想：我们现在怎么赢？我怎样才能更有创意、更有竞争力地找到下一个机会？我又怎样利用这些 always on（常开）、基于 loop 和 architecture（架构）的自动化，让 Yash 可以去做那些真正让他兴奋的事？我甚至都能想象 Yash 在 Slack 里说：“如果我们做一个三小时限时折扣，专门针对这些用户，配上这个视频，再用 Framer 搭这个东西，会怎么样？”而这种事其实一直都是这样，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: Like that work is the most fun way to work.
It's how smart, creative people are able to differentiate themselves.
But I think that the builder pack allows is that it allows someone like Josh to automate and defer a lot of the work to cloud agents.
He would have otherwise had to manually click in post hoc or manually code himself, and then he can go be creative on the space where he is an expertise.

And then it also allows someone like me who doesn't know what the fuck any of that stuff is, but kind of is naturally more of an ideas person to give the model and the SaaS tools in the builder pack my outcomes and ideas, and then just, like, let it run, which is, like, the craziest part for me.

paragraphs.zh: 这种工作方式本来就是最有趣的工作方式，也是聪明、有创造力的人真正拉开差距的方法。但我觉得 builder pack 带来的，是它让像 Josh 这样的人可以把大量工作自动化，并且交给 cloud agents（云端 agent）去做——这些工作本来要么得他自己手动在 post hoc 里点，要么得他亲自写代码。这样一来，他就可以把精力放到自己真正擅长、能够发挥专业能力的创造性空间里。与此同时，它也让像我这样的人——我根本搞不懂那一堆东西到底是什么鬼，但天然更偏 ideas person（点子型的人）——可以把我的结果目标和想法交给 model（模型）以及 builder pack 里的 SaaS tools（SaaS 工具），然后就让它自己跑起来。对我来说，这才是最疯狂的部分。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: Like, I I let go oh, yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 就是，我……我放手让它去做了。哦，对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 3

sentences.text: Can you tell that story of, you had an idea for something?
You went to bed.
You woke up.
You had four emails.

paragraphs.zh: 你能讲讲那个故事吗？就是你有了一个想法，然后去睡觉，醒来之后收到了四封 email。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: So it wasn't even my idea.
Like, we were we did the we did the builder pack launch on Tuesday.
It was going really well.
I'm on the West Coast.
Most of the team is on the East Coast.

paragraphs.zh: 其实那甚至都不是我的想法。我们周二做了 builder pack 的发布，进展非常顺利。我人在 West Coast，团队大部分人在 East Coast。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: As they were, like, getting ready to go to bed, Josh dropped a note in Slack saying, hey, we should send emails in the morning to a group of users who have shown some sort of checkout intent but not converted because we were running a early bird discount for the first twenty four hours of launch.
So it's like we should probably hit them again with some sort of urgency backed approach.
And then Yosh said, this isn't actually my idea, it's Dan's idea, but I'm sharing it.
And then they all signed off.
And I think maybe like 7PM here, I had been in front of my computer all day, and I really wanted to go to the gym.

paragraphs.zh: 当时他们差不多准备睡觉了，Josh 在 Slack 里留了一条消息，说，嘿，我们明早应该给一批用户发 email——这些用户已经表现出某种 checkout intent（结账意图），但还没有转化，因为我们在发布后的前二十四小时做了 early bird discount（早鸟折扣）。所以感觉我们应该再触达他们一次，用某种更有紧迫感的方式来推进。然后 Yosh 说，这其实不是我的主意，是 Dan 的主意，但我来转发一下。接着他们就都下线了。我这边大概晚上 7 点，我已经一整天都坐在电脑前了，特别想去健身房。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 5

sentences.text: And so I I took a screenshot of the Slack because it was like a bunch of different Slack messages.
Usually, would just like copy paste the link to the Slack message and send it to Codex.
I took a screenshot of the Slack.
